title: Euler Systems in the Twisted Friedberg-Jacquet Setting
speaker: Shilin Lai
abstract: We construct the tame part of a split anticyclotomic Euler system in a setting where local multiplicity one does not hold. Instead of the traditional zeta integral approach, we prove the existence of the necessary test vectors using a new method based on the unramified Plancherel formula. This is joint work with Li Cai and Yangyu Fan.

proof_stages.label: Twisted Friedberg–Jacquet period and L-value conjecture
proof_stages.summary: The talk recalls the unitary Friedberg–Jacquet period for a CM extension, explains why nonvanishing must be formulated over suitable inner or twisted forms, and introduces the biquadratic twisted setting in which two relevant L-functions appear.

proof_stages.label: Shimura-cycle arithmetic avatar
proof_stages.summary: Under a signature condition giving Shimura varieties of the right dimension, the Friedberg–Jacquet period problem is translated into the existence and arithmetic significance of a middle-dimensional special cycle whose cohomology sees the Galois representation attached to the automorphic representation.

proof_stages.label: Split anticyclotomic Euler-system statement
proof_stages.summary: The main question and result are formulated: the base special-cycle class is extended to a split anticyclotomic Euler system for the restricted representation, with classes over ring class fields satisfying a Hecke-polynomial corestriction relation.

proof_stages.label: Global cycle classes reduced to test functions
proof_stages.summary: The geometric construction of higher classes is reduced to constructing compactly supported test functions on an augmented quotient involving a U(1) factor, where the U(1) level records anticyclotomic fields and the desired Euler-system norm relation becomes a trace identity for a function delta_l.

proof_stages.label: Local spherical-variety trace problem
proof_stages.summary: After localizing at a split place l of the CM field, the problem becomes a purely local harmonic-analysis question on a spherical variety, with separate split-split and inert-unitary local forms but a common desired trace relation in a compactly supported function space.

proof_stages.label: Trace-image criterion from asymptotic Cartan geometry
proof_stages.summary: The image of the trace map is characterized using a generalized Cartan decomposition and asymptotic degeneration of the spherical variety, yielding a sufficient criterion: divisibility by l minus 1 along the distinguished type-T wall.

proof_stages.label: Satake-transform inversion for the Hecke polynomial
proof_stages.summary: To check the trace-image criterion for the Hecke polynomial applied to the basic function, the speaker avoids an explicit double-coset computation by using a Satake isomorphism and an inversion formula expressing coefficients through a concrete Laurent expansion kernel.

proof_stages.label: Type-T antisymmetry and divisibility conclusion
proof_stages.summary: The Laurent-kernel formula is specialized at l equals 1; cancellations and Weyl antisymmetry across type-T walls force the relevant wall coefficient to vanish modulo l minus 1, proving the trace criterion and hence producing the required local functions and Euler-system classes.

examples.name: Gan–Gross–Prasad inner-form analogy
examples.note: The Gan–Gross–Prasad case is invoked to show why a naive single-period/nonzero-L-value equivalence must be replaced by a statement ranging over inner forms, motivating the twisted Friedberg–Jacquet formulation.
examples.engagement_reason: The Gan–Gross–Prasad case is cited only as an analogy for needing to consider inner forms, with no computation or construction carried out in that case.

passes.reason: The GGP inner-form phenomenon is a systematic family used to show the naive single-inner-form period/L-value equivalence fails.
Used to show the naive single-inner-form period/L-value formulation is insufficient already in the Gan–Gross–Prasad setting.
Standard GGP inner-form setting is cited to show the naive single-inner-form period/L-value equivalence fails.
The GGP inner-form phenomenon is a standard period-theoretic setting, invoked to show the naive fixed-inner-form period equivalence fails.
Invoked as a systematic period/L-value setting showing the naive single-inner-form formulation fails and must range over inner forms.
Used to show that a naive single-inner-form period/L-value statement fails already in the GGP setting; the setting itself is a systematic family, not structurally exceptional.
the GGP inner-form phenomenon is invoked to show the naive single-inner-form period/L-value statement cannot hold

Gan–Gross–Prasad is invoked to show the naive single-inner-form period/L-value equivalence fails, while the setting itself is a systematic period problem rather than a degenerate, extremal, exceptional, or maximally symmetric object.
used as a precedent showing that a naive single-inner-form period criterion fails and must be replaced by an inner-form statement

used to show the naive single-inner-form period/nonzero-central-value equivalence fails; GGP is a systematic classical-family setting, not sporadic, extremal, degenerate, or maximally symmetric as an object here
Invoked to show the naïve fixed-inner-form period/L-value equivalence fails and must be replaced by a statement varying inner forms.

Used to show the naive single-inner-form period/nonvanishing equivalence fails, since GGP already requires considering inner forms; the GGP setting itself is a standard family, not sporadic, limiting, extremal, or maximally symmetric.

examples.name: Classical Cartan decomposition as model case
examples.note: The usual Cartan decomposition for a group with maximal compact subgroups is mentioned as the familiar special case of the generalized Cartan decomposition used to index the quotient space by a lattice.
examples.engagement_reason: The classical Cartan decomposition is invoked as the model for a generalized decomposition and its indexing, but the speaker does not work through the classical case concretely.

passes.reason: The classical Cartan case is the highly symmetric group/double-coset model used illustratively for the generalized decomposition, not to refute a claim.
The classical Cartan decomposition is invoked as the highly regular left-right group model for the generalized decomposition, not to refute a claim.
The group-case Cartan decomposition is a highly symmetric homogeneous model used only as a template for the generalized decomposition.
The classical Cartan decomposition is used as an ordinary model for the generalized decomposition, not to refute a claim or as a structurally exceptional case.
Used as the highly regular group-case prototype for the generalized Cartan decomposition, not to refute a claim.
The classical Cartan decomposition is invoked as the regular model for the generalized decomposition, not as an obstruction or sporadic/extreme case.
the classical Cartan decomposition is used as a standard symmetric group-case model for the generalized decomposition
The classical Cartan decomposition is used as a template for the generalized decomposition, and the underlying group double-coset situation is the prototypical highly regular symmetric model.
used as the standard model for the generalized Cartan decomposition, with the group/symmetric-space case having maximal regularity

cited as the standard model for the generalized Cartan decomposition rather than to refute a claim; the classical decomposition is a regular representative case, not a classification exception, boundary case, or collapse
Used as the standard model for the generalized decomposition, with the group case intrinsically a highly regular symmetric homogeneous space.

Used as an explanatory model for the generalized Cartan decomposition, and the classical double-coset decomposition comes from the highly regular symmetric-space setup rather than serving as an obstruction.
